project.name: dotfiles
project.description: Opinionated Bash/Zsh dotfiles for reproducible terminal environments across WSL, Linux, and macOS. A modular shell-config system: shared POSIX code in shell-common/ is sourced by both bash/ and zsh/ loaders; git/ manages a 2-tier hook system; tests/ holds bats + pytest suites.

nodes.name: plugin-sync-session.sh
nodes.summary: SessionStart/Stop hook that snapshots the shared plugin SSOT at session start and, on Stop, detects hash/key-set drift to drive plugin-sync.sh's add and remove branches — covering slash-command plugin mutations that emit no Bash tool call.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses jq for JSON hashing/key-set diffing and a sha256sum/shasum fallback for macOS portability; always exits 0 as a best-effort hook.

nodes.name: _snapshot
nodes.summary: Emits a JSON baseline of the plugin SSOT — sha256 hashes plus marketplace/plugin key sets for known_marketplaces.json and installed_plugins.json — used to detect additions and removals between session start and stop.

nodes.name: plugin-sync.sh

nodes.languageNotes: Heavy use of jq for union-merge manifest reconciliation plus awk-based argument extraction; guards commits behind change detection and protected-branch push logic.

nodes.name: _extract_target
nodes.summary: Parses the first non-flag argument following a keyword alternation (e.g. install/uninstall/marketplace) so intervening flags like --yes are not mistaken for the plugin/marketplace target.

nodes.name: _commit_if_changed
nodes.summary: Stages the plugin manifest files and creates a single sync commit only when the working tree actually changed, keeping the manifest history free of no-op commits.

nodes.name: _push_if_protected
nodes.summary: Pushes the freshly created sync commit when the current branch is a protected/tracked branch, so manifest updates propagate without manual intervention.

nodes.name: post-gh-pr-create.sh
nodes.summary: PostToolUse hook for `gh pr create` that parses the new PR URL and syncs the GitHub project board — moving the PR card to "In review" and linked issues to "In progress" — so exactly one writer handles board sync per environment.
nodes.languageNotes: Best-effort exit 0 with jq gating; delegates board status writes to a shared _gh_project_status_sync helper with a --only-from guard to avoid regressing Done issues.

nodes.name: _post_gh_pr_create_repo_has_board
nodes.summary: Checks whether the current repository has an associated GitHub project board before attempting any board status sync, short-circuiting the hook for boardless repos.

nodes.name: session-start-pc-context.sh
nodes.summary: SessionStart hook that injects the current PC's dotfiles setup-mode (public/internal/external) and hostname into the session as additionalContext, giving each machine a per-host fact that shared CLAUDE.md cannot hold.
nodes.languageNotes: Canonicalizes legacy numeric mode values 1/2/3 to public/internal/external and emits hookSpecificOutput JSON via jq, falling back to plain text when jq is absent.

nodes.name: session-start-plugin-path-normalize.sh
nodes.summary: SessionStart hook that re-stamps plugin installLocation/installPath fields in the shared plugin SSOT to the active $CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR spelling, working around Claude Code 2.1.199+'s literal string-prefix validation across symlinked multi-account plugin dirs.
nodes.languageNotes: Idempotent — rewrites and backs up a file only when content changes; scopes rewrites to ~/.claude*/plugins prefixes and registers before plugin-sync-session.sh so its baseline captures normalized bytes.

nodes.name: _normalize_file
nodes.summary: Rewrites the plugin path fields of a single SSOT JSON file to the active config-dir spelling, backing up and writing only when the content actually changed.

nodes.name: session-start-settings-drift.sh
nodes.summary: SessionStart hook that warns when the live settings.json `.hooks` block has drifted from the dotfiles SSOT (claude/settings.json), catching the case where a user git-pulls new hooks but never re-runs setup.sh so the hooks silently never fire.
nodes.languageNotes: Compares only the `.hooks` jq subtree to stay immune to the Bedrock env/model overlay; best-effort exit 0, and cannot warn about its own first install.

layers.description: POSIX-compatible shared shell code (env, aliases, functions, ux_lib, tool integrations, hooks) sourced by both the bash and zsh loaders.

layers.name: Shell Entry Points
layers.description: Bash- and Zsh-specific entry points (main.bash, main.zsh), per-shell env, and app configuration that bootstrap each shell.

layers.name: Git Integration
layers.description: Git configuration, the 2-tier hook system with SSOT config and pre-push leak guards, plus root-level git dotfiles (.gitmessage, .gitmodules, .gitattributes).

layers.name: Claude/AI Integration
layers.description: Claude Code integration: skills, plugin manifests, statusline, docs, and settings symlinked into each account config directory.

layers.name: Installation & Tooling
layers.description: Setup/install orchestrators, maintenance scripts, tool-specific setup (aws), and per-package-manager configuration (apt, npm, pip, cargo, etc.) plus root build/lint config.

layers.name: Documentation
layers.description: Project documentation tree (ADRs, guides, requirements, feature specs, superpowers) plus root README/AGENTS/CLAUDE overview docs.

layers.name: Tests
layers.description: bats function tests, pytest integration suites, and golden-rule conformance checks with their fixtures and helpers.

layers.name: CI/CD
layers.description: GitHub Actions workflows for lint, test, CLI packaging, and project-board synchronization.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the README to understand what this repository is: an opinionated, modular Bash/Zsh dotfiles system for reproducible terminal environments across WSL, Linux, and macOS. It lays out the three-layer shell design (bash/, zsh/, and shared shell-common/), the environment-specific setup flow, and the quick-start that runs setup.sh. Keep this mental map handy — every later step is one of the components the README names.

tour.title: The Setup Orchestrator
tour.description: setup.sh is the true entry point of the project: running it is how a fresh machine becomes a configured environment. It asks for the environment (Home/Internal/External), writes the .local.sh config files, and then delegates to the per-module setup scripts before creating the shell symlinks. Read this first to see the whole installation story from the top down.
tour.languageLesson: Shell orchestrator scripts compose smaller scripts by sourcing or invoking them in sequence. Keeping each module's setup.sh independent lets the top-level script stay a thin coordinator rather than a monolith.

tour.title: Module Setup Scripts
tour.description: Building on the orchestrator from Step 2, each layer owns its own setup.sh that the top-level script calls. shell-common/setup.sh provisions per-tool configuration (npm, uv, pip, cargo, claude, and more), while bash/setup.sh and zsh/setup.sh each install their shell's symlinks with logging, backup of existing files, and idempotent re-runs. This is where the README's 'creates shell symlinks' step actually happens.

tour.title: Shell Entry Points
tour.description: Once symlinks exist, ~/.bashrc and ~/.zshrc point at these loaders — the code that runs on every new interactive shell. main.bash and main.zsh each guard against non-interactive shells, then source the shared shell-common code before layering their shell-specific env and app configuration on top. This is the runtime counterpart to the install-time scripts from Step 3.
tour.languageLesson: Every output-producing file begins with an interactive guard — `case $- in *i*) ;; *) return 0 ;; esac` — so sourcing the file in a non-interactive shell (a script, a hook) exits early and prints nothing.

tour.title: The UX Library
tour.description: ux_lib.sh is the most widely depended-upon file in the codebase — nearly every app script sources it. It provides all colored, structured terminal output primitives (ux_header, ux_success, ux_error, ux_info), and the project rule is that scripts must use these instead of raw echo/printf/tput. Understanding this library early makes every later script readable, because you will recognize its output vocabulary everywhere.

tour.title: Commands and the Help Registry
tour.description: The user-facing commands live in shell-common/functions. mytool.sh is a collection of personal utilities (hardware info, source packing, agent init), and my_help.sh is the centralized help registry that every command registers into, giving the dotfiles a single discoverable help surface. Both build directly on the UX library from Step 5 for their output.

tour.title: The Git Worktree Toolkit
tour.description: git_worktree.sh (the `gwt` command) is one of the largest and highest-fan-out functions in the repo: a full worktree lifecycle toolkit — add, list, status, prune, remove, and spawn. It exemplifies how a substantial feature is packaged as a shell-common function that leans on ux_lib for presentation and integrates with the git tooling covered next.

tour.title: Git Configuration & Hook System
tour.description: The git/ module contributes the .gitconfig symlink (via git/setup.sh from Step 3) and a 2-tier hook system driven entirely by SSOT config. hook-config.sh holds the regex patterns and thresholds, pre-push-rules.sh defines the protected-branch list and upstream leak-guard patterns, and the pre-push hook enforces them. This centralized-config-plus-thin-hook pattern is a recurring theme in the project.
tour.languageLesson: Separating an SSOT config file (data) from the hook script (logic) means behavior changes are edits to a pattern list, not to executable code — safer to review and easier to test in isolation.

tour.title: Claude Code / AI Integration
tour.description: The claude integration threads through both shell and config layers. tools/integrations/claude.sh handles multi-account setup, settings management, and symlinking Claude config into each account directory, while settings.json is the SSOT for env vars, lifecycle hooks, and the status line rendered by statusline-command.sh. The plugin marketplaces manifest declares which Claude Code plugin sources are available. Together these show how the dotfiles manage an external AI tool as a first-class module.

tour.title: Build, Lint & Test Configuration
tour.description: Code quality — one of the README's headline features — is wired through mise and Python tooling. mise.toml is the command SSOT defining the canonical lint/fix/test tasks, and pyproject.toml declares the Python build backend plus ruff and mypy settings. These are the files behind the `mise run lint` and `mise run test` commands you would run while developing in this repo.
tour.languageLesson: mise.toml centralizes tool versions, environment variables, and task definitions in one file, so `mise run <task>` is the single portable command surface across machines — no per-developer Makefile drift.

tour.title: Contributor Guidance
tour.description: The AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md documents encode the project's operating rules for both humans and AI agents: the three-layer architecture, POSIX/cross-shell golden rules, directory-placement decision trees, and the critical constraints (interactive guards, no direct ~/.bashrc writes, ux_lib-only output). Reading these ties together the patterns you saw enforced in the code across Steps 4-10.

tour.title: Continuous Integration
tour.description: As a capstone, the GitHub Actions workflows show how the quality gates from Step 10 run automatically. ci.yml runs the lint and test jobs on every push and pull request with concurrency cancellation for superseded runs, and test.yml focuses on executing the repository's bats + pytest + golden-rule suite. This closes the loop from local `mise run test` to enforced CI.
tour.languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows use `on` triggers to decide when to run and `jobs` to run work in parallel. A concurrency group that cancels in-progress runs saves CI minutes when commits land faster than builds finish.
